turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court in case 17-312, United States v. Sanchez-Gomez. United States Marshals Service is responsible for safely presenting defendants for hearings in Federal Criminal Proceedings. In 2013 the United States Marshal for the Southern District of California asked the judges of that district to permit the use of full restraints on all defendants during pretrial hearings. When full restraints are used a defendant's hands are closely handcuffed together. The handcuffs are connected by a chain to another chain running around the defendant's waist and the defendant's feet are shackled and chained together. The judges agreed to the Marshal's request with a modification allowing a Judge to require that a detainee be produced without the restraints. Now the respondents in this case are four defendants who were presented in full restraints for pretrial proceedings in their criminal cases. They argued that the restraint policy was unconstitutional. The District Court denied their challenges and they appealed to the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. While their appeals were pending and before the Court of Appeals could issue a decision the criminal cases of all four respondents came to an end. Three respondents pled guilty and the charges against the fourth were dismissed. The termination of their criminal cases meant that respondents would not have to appear again in court for pretrial proceedings in full restraints. Now normally when something like this happens the case is moot and the Court of Appeals is not supposed to decide it. That is because the Constitution permits the federal courts to decide only cases in which the parties still have a stake in the outcome. Now that is important because the only reason federal courts can construe the Constitution and say what the law is, is because it is necessary to do so to decide a case. If there is no real case because one side has no stake in the outcome there is no authority to construe the Constitution. But the Court of Appeals decided respondents' cases anyway. That court explained that even if respondents were no longer subject to appearing in court in full restraints their challenge to the restraint policy could still benefit other criminal defendants. According to the Court of Appeals that made respondents' case a "functional class action." The Court of Appeals then looked to some of this Court's cases involving actual civil class actions and held that on the basis of those precedents respondents' case was not moot. That was an error. The class action is a special exception in civil cases to the usual rule that people can only litigate claims for themselves and not for other people who are not parties to the case. In a class action a named plaintiff files claims on his own behalf but also on behalf of a class of similarly situated people. Later on, if appropriate, the judge formally certifies the class. When certification happens the class members are for many purposes actual parties to the case. The certified class has what we have called an Independent Legal Status in the case. Now because of the special features we have held that a class-action may often go on even if the named plaintiff's individual claim becomes moot so long as the District Court properly certified a class. But that rule we have emphasized is only for class actions, it does not apply here. Unlike in civil cases there is no such thing as a class action in criminal cases. The Court of Appeals was therefore wrong to apply our civil class action precedents to save respondents' appeals in their criminal cases from mootness. But the respondents argue that there is a different exception to mootness that applies to them. It is the exception for claims that are capable of repetition yet evading review. It applies only when there is a reasonable expectation that the challenger will be subjected to the challenged action again in the future. Two respondents Rene Sanchez-Gomez and Moises Patricio-Guzman say that they satisfy that requirement because they are likely to illegally enter the United States again, be apprehended, be returned to pretrial custody and have to appear in court in full restraints. In other words, if we dismiss this case as moot they will be right back in court where they started. Now the problem with that argument is that we have consistently refused to conclude that a case is not moot solely because the party claims he is going to violate valid criminal laws again. Respondents contend that the facts of this case ward an exception from that usual rule. They point out that we have twice in civil cases called Honig v. Doe and Turner v. Rogers, decided that cases challenging government conduct were not moot because the facts made clear that the litigants would again engage in prohibited conduct and be subjected to the challenge government action. According to respondents the same is true here. Sanchez-Gomez and Patricio-Guzman were prosecuted for offenses related to their illegal entry into the United States. There is statistical evidence that people who commit one illegal entry offense are likely to commit another one in the future. And after their convictions in this case both Sanchez-Gomez and Patricio-Guzman actually did cross the border again illegally were apprehended, were charged with new illegal entry offenses. But in the cases they had cited, Honig and Turner are decisions rested on the fact that the litigants were unable for reasons beyond their control to prevent themselves from offending again. Honig involved the student whose mental condition effectively prevented him from controlling his behavior and Turner involved someone found in contempt for failure to pay child support who was $16,000 in arrears, unemployed and faced with addiction issues and who would almost certainly miss the next eminent payment and face contempt again. Here in contrast, Sanchez-Gomez and Patricio-Guzman have not given us any reason to believe that they are different from other criminal defendants and unable to obey the law. We think that the general rule against presuming future criminal conduct applies and that the case is moot. Now this is not to say that there is no way for detainees to obtain judicial resolution of their constitutional claims. In the course of this litigation several possible options were discussed. We expressed no view on those. In some we vacate the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit and remand the case to that court with instructions to dismiss as moot. Our opinion is unanimous.
